1: vayigshu rashei avot haLviim el Eleazar hakohen: ‘the heads of the Levitical fathers came to Eleazar the priest’ — the petition is presented to the right authority (Eleazar the priest, not Joshua alone), consistent with Numbers 35’s assignment of the Levitical cities as a priestly matter. The Levites’ request initiates the last major land-distribution act. Unlike the tribal allotments, the Levitical cities are not a homeland but a dispersal: forty-eight cities spread through all twelve tribes, so that the priestly service function is distributed across the entire nation. The design prevents the concentration of priestly power in one location while ensuring that every tribe has access to Levitical instruction.
2: tzavah YHWH beyad Moshe latet lanu arim lashevet umigrasheichen: ‘YHWH commanded through Moses to give us cities to live in, with pasturelands for our livestock’ — the petition is grounded in prior divine command (Numbers 35:1-8), not in general need. The Levites are not asking for generosity but invoking an obligation: YHWH promised this through Moses. The migrash (pastureland, grazing area) attached to each city is a technical land term — a strip of common land surrounding the city, not for agriculture but for livestock. The Levites’ material provision is specified to prevent their dependence on tribal agriculture while preserving their availability for priestly service.
3: vayitnu bnei Yisrael laLviim min nachalatam al pi YHWH: ‘the Israelites gave the Levites from their inheritance at YHWH’s command’ — the giving is from tribal allotments: each tribe contributes cities to the Levites from land already apportioned to them. This is the mechanism of Numbers 35:8 (in proportion to the inheritance each tribe receives, so the cities it gives). The obedience formula al pi YHWH (at the mouth of YHWH) marks the transaction as covenant compliance, not tribal generosity. Israel is not doing the Levites a favor but honoring a divine arrangement.
4: vayetze hagoral lemishpachot haQehati: ‘the lot fell for the Kohathite families’ — the three Levitical clans (Kohath, Gershon, Merari) receive their cities by lot, introducing the element of divine determination into the geographic distribution. The Kohathites (Moses and Aaron’s clan) receive first; within the Kohathites, the Aaronite priestly line receives thirteen cities from Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin — the southern tribes closest to Jerusalem, anticipating the future centralization of priestly service at the Davidic temple. The distribution is not random but providentially positioned.
5: The remaining Kohathite families (non-priestly Kohathites who carried the ark and furnishings in the wilderness) received ten cities from Ephraim, Dan, and half-Manasseh. The geographic distribution of non-priestly Kohathites in the central and northern territories corresponds to the areas with the highest subsequent need for Levitical teaching. The later turbulence of the northern kingdom (1 Kings 12:31 — Jeroboam’s non-Levitical priests) will confirm that the loss of Levitical presence had devastating consequences.
6: The Gershonites received thirteen cities from Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in Bashan — the northern and Transjordan territories. The Gershonites had carried the tabernacle’s coverings and curtains in the wilderness; their placement in the north distributes the priestly tradition through the tribal areas most geographically distant from the future temple. The provision for Levites in remote areas is an infrastructure decision: the covenant is not a Jerusalem-only institution.
7: The Merarites received twelve cities from Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun — spanning both sides of the Jordan. The Merarites carried the tabernacle’s structural components (boards, bars, pillars) in the wilderness; their placement in the Transjordan tribes and the northern plain ensures that the tribal territories that would otherwise have the weakest Levitical connection are served. The geographic intentionality of the distribution becomes apparent when mapped: no tribal territory is without Levitical cities.
8: vayitnu bnei Yisrael et he'arim ha'eleh vegidrotheichen laLviim kal asher tzivah YHWH beyad Moshe: ‘the Israelites gave these cities and their pasturelands to the Levites, just as YHWH commanded through Moses’ — summary formula closing the introduction before the detailed city lists. The double attribution — to Israel’s act and to YHWH’s prior command — is characteristic of Joshua’s narrative theology: human obedience and divine institution are simultaneous, not competing.
9: The city list begins with Judah and Simeon — the tribes whose territory overlaps and who together provide the first set of Aaronite cities. The narrative precision of the city lists (naming each city individually rather than describing regions) creates a verifiable record: the Levitical distribution is not a literary ideal but a documented historical reality. The specificity also serves a mnemonic function: these cities with their Levitical connections were part of Israel’s institutional memory.
10: The descendants of Aaron receive the first lot — the priestly line within the Kohathites. The narrative re-identifies them as Kohathite Levites to clarify the clan structure: all Aaronites are Kohathites, but not all Kohathites are Aaronites. The structural precision matters because the distinction between priestly Kohathites (Aaron’s line) and non-priestly Kohathites (other Kohathite families) is the fundamental division of Levitical function: proximity to the altar is reserved for Aaron’s sons.
11: et Qiryat Arba avi haAnak hi Chevron: ‘Kiriath-arba, which is Hebron — Arba was the forefather of Anak’ — Hebron’s double identification (both names, with genealogical note) is consistent with Joshua’s practice of noting the pre-Israelite names of significant sites. Hebron is significant because it was Caleb’s personal allotment (Joshua 14:13-14) and David’s first capital (2 Samuel 2:1-4). The Levitical city and Caleb’s personal holding coexist: the city itself (with its pasturelands) goes to the Levites; the fields and villages of the surrounding territory go to Caleb (v12).
12: vesedeh hair vegidroteiha natan Calev ben Yefuneh biachuzato: ‘but the fields and villages they gave to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his personal possession’ — the distinction between the city proper (including its immediate pasturelands) and the surrounding agricultural territory is precise. Caleb retains his promised inheritance; the Levites receive the city as a residence and service base. The two claims do not conflict because they operate at different scales: the city for Levitical habitation, the territory for Calebite agriculture. The careful demarcation reflects the actual administrative complexity of land tenure in ancient Israel.
13: uvnei Aharon hakohen natan et Chevron ir miqlashot haroteach: ‘to the descendants of Aaron the priest they gave Hebron — a city of refuge for the manslayer’ — the overlap of Levitical city and city of refuge is deliberate: in the priestly territories, the places of asylum are also places of priestly presence. The manslayer who flees to a city of refuge is in a space where priestly mediation is immediately available — not merely protected from the blood-avenger, but in the presence of those who can adjudicate the case and provide atonement.
14: Jattir and Eshtemoa with their pasturelands — cities in the southern hill country of Judah. The names of these cities become part of the record of David’s distribution of plunder to the elders of Judah after Ziklag (1 Samuel 30:27-28): Eshtemoa appears in that list, connecting the later Davidic history to the Levitical city network. The Levitical cities were centers of community life, not isolated priestly enclaves.
15: Holon and Debir — cities in the Judahite hill country. Debir (Kiriath-sepher) was taken by Othniel, Caleb’s nephew, as the first judge-act of the conquest period (Joshua 15:15-19). Its assignment here as a Levitical city means that the site of the first conquest-by-faith becomes a center of priestly presence and instruction. The geography of redemptive history accumulates meaning: places where YHWH acted become places where YHWH is taught.
16: Ain, Juttah, Beth-shemesh — completing the nine cities from Judah and Simeon for the Aaronite priests. Beth-shemesh (House of the Sun) will later be the destination of the ark when it is returned by the Philistines (1 Samuel 6:12-19). The event at Beth-shemesh — where men were struck dead for looking into the ark — demonstrates that the Levitical city designation did not guarantee correct priestly practice; the presence of a Levitical city is the institutional provision, but faithfulness to its covenant function is always contingent.
17: Gibeon and Geba from Benjamin — significant sites. Gibeon was the city of the Hivite deception (Joshua 9) and will become the location of the tabernacle during David’s reign (1 Chronicles 16:39; Joshua 21:29). Geba is a strategic pass in Benjamin’s territory that will appear in later military narratives. The Levitical cities are often at geographic and strategic crossroads — not coincidentally, since the need for priestly presence and legal adjudication would be highest at places of significant movement and exchange.
18: Anathoth and Almon from Benjamin — Anathoth is the priestly city where Jeremiah was born (Jeremiah 1:1). The prophet’s identification as “the son of Hilkiah, of the priests who were in Anathoth” (Jeremiah 1:1) places his origin within the Levitical city network. Jeremiah’s conflict with the temple establishment and his radical critique of Judah’s covenant failures emerges from within the priestly tradition, not outside it — a priest’s son speaking covenant truth that the temple priests refused to hear.
19: kol arei bnei Aharon hakohanim shlosha esre ir umigrasheichen: ‘all the cities of the priestly line of Aaron: thirteen with their pasturelands’ — the summary closes the Aaronite section. Thirteen cities for the priests; the number is not round (unlike the Kohathite non-priestly ten, the Gershonite thirteen, and the Merarite twelve) because priestly cities follow tribal availability. The careful accounting — tribe by tribe, subtotal by subtotal — reflects a genuine administrative record of the Levitical distribution.
20: The non-priestly Kohathites begin their section. “The rest of the Kohathite Levites” distinguishes them from Aaron’s descendants: these are the families who carried the ark and the furniture of the inner sanctuary (Numbers 4:4-15). Their placement in Ephraim, Dan, and half-Manasseh positions the carriers of the ark in the central hill country, which will later be the territory of the divided monarchy’s northern kingdom — a providential distribution for the period when the ark’s significance will be most contested.
21: et Shekem ir miqlat laroteach behar Efrayim: ‘they gave them Shechem — a city of refuge — in the hill country of Ephraim’ — Shechem as a non-priestly Kohathite city and city of refuge places the second city of asylum in Ephraim, the dominant northern tribe. Shechem’s long history (Genesis 12:6, Joshua 33:18, Joshua 34, the Dinah incident; Josh 24, the covenant renewal) makes it a natural center for the Levitical presence in the north. The assembly of Israel in Joshua 24 will gather here, and the Levitical presence is the institutional framework within which that assembly is possible.
22: Kibzaim and Beth-horon from Ephraim — four cities total for the non-priestly Kohathites in Ephraim. Beth-horon (both Upper and Lower) is a strategic pass connecting the central hills to the coastal plain and the site of significant military actions (Joshua 10:10-11; 1 Samuel 13:18; 1 Macc Joshua 3:16). Levitical cities at strategic passes serve as waypoints of priestly presence along major travel routes.
23: Eltekeh and Gibbethon from Dan — cities in the Shephelah (lowland) region between the hill country and the coast. Dan’s territory was notoriously difficult to hold (Joshua 15:63; Judges 1:34) and eventually caused the Danite migration northward (Judg 18). The Levitical cities in Dan were in a contested zone, and the tribe’s failure to control its territory meant the Levitical infrastructure there was compromised from early on.
24: Aijalon and Gath-rimmon from Dan — completing the four Danite cities for the non-priestly Kohathites. Aijalon is the valley where the sun stood still (Joshua 10:12). The connection between miraculous divine intervention and Levitical city is characteristic of Joshua’s narrative: the sites of great acts become places where the covenant is taught and the acts are remembered.
25: Taanach and Gath-rimmon from half-Manasseh — two cities. The name Gath-rimmon appears twice in vv24-25 (from Dan and from half-Manasseh) and 1 Chronicles 6:69 changes the Manassite city to Ibleam, suggesting a scribal error in one of the two occurrences. The textual variant is one of the few points where the Levitical city lists show divergence between Joshua and 1 Chronicles 6, providing evidence for copyist variation in lists of geographic names.
26: kol arim asarah umigrasheichen limishpachot bnei Qehat hanotirim: ‘all the cities of the remaining Kohathite families: ten with their pasturelands’ — the subtotal closes the non-priestly Kohathite section. Ten cities for the carrying-families of the inner sanctuary, plus thirteen for the Aaronite priests, equals twenty-three Kohathite cities total — almost half the forty-eight. The disproportionate Kohathite share reflects their double function: priestly service (Aaron’s line) and ark-bearing (the rest of Kohath).
27: ulivnei Gershon mimishpachot haLevi minchatzi shevet haMenashe et Golan baBashan ir miqlat: ‘to the Gershonite Levites, from the other half-tribe of Manasseh: Golan in Bashan — a city of refuge’ — Golan in Bashan is the third city of refuge (of six), placed in the Transjordan. The Gershonites receive the northern and Transjordan territory, distributing the priestly tradition to the areas furthest from the future Jerusalem temple. Golan’s modern prominence (the Golan Heights) reflects its strategic importance, which ancient Israel’s land distribution recognized by placing a Levitical city there.
28: Kishon and Daberath from Issachar — cities in the Jezreel Valley. The Jezreel Valley is one of the most fertile and strategically significant regions of ancient Canaan, the site of the battle of Megiddo (Judg 5) and numerous subsequent military engagements. Levitical cities in the Jezreel Valley place priestly presence at the agricultural and military heart of the northern territory.
29: Jarmuth and En-gannim from Issachar — completing the four Issachar cities for the Gershonites. En-gannim (“spring of gardens”) near the Jezreel Valley’s access point continues the pattern of Levitical cities at geographically significant locations. The spring (ein) names in the Levitical city list reflect the practical requirement that communities of any size need reliable water sources — a mundane material consideration woven into the sacred institutional structure.
30: Mishal and Abdon from Asher — cities in the coastal foothills of the northern coastal territory. Asher’s territory along the Phoenician coast was notoriously difficult to secure from indigenous populations (Judges 1:31-32), and the Levitical cities in Asher were in a mixed zone where the covenant instruction would be most needed and most challenged.
31: Helkath and Rehob from Asher — completing the four Asher cities. The Gershonite cities in Asher and Naphtali position the priestly carrying-families in the northernmost tribal territories, the region that will first fall to Assyrian conquest (2 Kings 15:29). The Levitical network in the north is both the institutional provision for those territories and a measure of what was lost when Assyria depopulated them.
32: veQedesh baGalil ir miqlat laroteach: ‘Kedesh in Galilee — a city of refuge’ — Kedesh of Galilee is the fourth city of refuge, in the northern territory that Jesus will inhabit (“Galilee of the Gentiles”, Matthew 4:15). The city of refuge in Galilee makes the northern territory, with its highest concentration of non-Israelite population, a place where the asylum principle applies. The geographic reach of the miqlat cities — from Hebron in the south to Kedesh in the far north, and from Bezer in the Transjordan to Shechem in the central hill country — reflects the intent that the asylum provision cover all of the land.
33: kol arei haGershuni lemishpachtam shlosha asher ir umigrasheichen: ‘all the cities of the Gershonites by their families: thirteen with their pasturelands’ — the Gershonite subtotal matches the Aaronite total (thirteen each), though covering a different geographic range. The northern distribution of the Gershonites creates a priestly presence network across the territories that will later constitute the northern kingdom of Israel — where the eight Levitical cities of the Gershonites should have been centers of covenant instruction preventing the Jeroboam apostasy.
34: The Merarites receive cities from Zebulun — beginning the last Levitical clan’s distribution. The Merarites carried the structural components of the tabernacle (boards, bars, pillars, bases) in the wilderness — the heaviest loads, the load-bearing elements. Their cities in Zebulun and Reuben and Gad cover the tribal territories flanking the Jezreel Valley on the north and the Transjordan. Jokneam is at the southwestern entrance to the Jezreel Valley; Kartah is uncertain in location. The structural-carrier families settle in the structural hinge-points of the land’s geography.
35: Dimnah and Nahalal from Zebulun — completing the four Zebulun cities for the Merarites. Nahalal is the site from which Zebulun could not drive out the Canaanites (Judges 1:30); a Levitical city in a zone of incomplete conquest is a measure of the ongoing challenge of covenant faithfulness in mixed zones. The Levitical infrastructure existed; the tribal will to maintain separation was inconsistent.
36: Bezer and Jahaz from Reuben — Bezer being the fifth city of refuge, in the Transjordan. Bezer (bezer = ore/strong place) in the wilderness of the plateau was the city of asylum for the Transjordan region. Jahaz was the site of Israel’s victory over Sihon king of the Amorites (Numbers 21:23) — the battle that opened the Transjordan conquest. The location of a Levitical city at a site of decisive divine victory is consistent with the pattern of placing priestly instruction at sites where YHWH acted decisively.
37: Kedemoth and Mephaath from Reuben — completing the four Reubenite cities. Kedemoth is associated with Moses’s wilderness preaching (Deuteronomy 2:26) and the failed negotiation with Sihon. The accumulation of historic associations in the Levitical city names is significant: these are not arbitrary geographic assignments but sites whose histories are intertwined with Israel’s foundational narrative. The Levitical presence in these cities connects the priestly instruction to the remembered acts of YHWH.
38: umimat Gad Ramot baGilad ir miqlat laroteach: ‘from Gad: Ramoth in Gilead — a city of refuge’ — Ramoth Gilead is the sixth and last city of refuge, in the Gadite territory of the Transjordan. Ramoth Gilead will later be the site of repeated Israelite-Aramean conflict (1 Kings 22:1-38; 2 Kings 8:28-29; Joshua 9:1-15), the city Ahab dies trying to reclaim and where Elijah anoints Jehu. The city of refuge that was to protect the innocent becomes the site of Ahab’s unjust death — irony within the book’s later history.
39: Heshbon and Jazer from Gad — Heshbon being the former capital of Sihon king of the Amorites, whose conquest opened the Transjordan (Numbers 21:25-26). The assignment of Sihon’s capital to the Merarites as a Levitical city is a transformative act: the seat of pagan kingship becomes a center of Levitical instruction. The same transformation principle governs the broader conquest narrative: places of pagan power are consecrated to YHWH’s service.
40: kol ha'arim livnei Merari lemishpachtam hanotirim mimmishpachot haLevi vayehi goralham arim shtem esreh: ‘all the cities assigned to the Merarites by their families — the remaining Levitical families — were twelve’ — the Merarite subtotal closes the clan-by-clan distribution. Twelve cities for the structural-carriers, completing the tallying that has proceeded with administrative precision through the chapter. The four subtotals (13 + 10 + 13 + 12 = 48) account for all the Levitical cities.
41: kol arei haLviim bithoch achuzat bnei Yisrael arbaim ushmone ir umigrasheichen: ‘the total number of Levitical cities within Israelite territory: forty-eight cities with their pasturelands’ — the grand total. Forty-eight is four times twelve: four clans (Aaron, non-priestly Kohath, Gershon, Merari) times twelve tribes (or a variant allocation), though the actual distribution is not uniform across tribes. The number’s divisibility by twelve creates a symmetry: the covenant community structured by twelve receives a priestly service network with the same numerical resonance. Numbers as structural markers carry theological weight in the priestly organization of Israel.
42: he'arim ha'eleh ir ir umigrasheichen saviv lahem ken lehol he'arim ha'eleh: ‘every one of these cities had its surrounding pasturelands — it was the same for all these cities’ — the uniformity clause closes the administrative section. The migrash (pastureland) attached to every Levitical city ensures that each one is functionally complete: the Levites can maintain their livestock and sustain their communities without dependence on individual tribal hospitality. The structural provision for the priestly tribe is comprehensive and equitable.
43: vayiten YHWH leYisrael et kol ha'aretz asher nishba latet la'avotam: ‘the LORD gave Israel all the land that he had sworn to give their ancestors’ — the first of three fulfillment declarations that close chapters 21-23. The land-promise to Abraham (Genesis 12:7; Joshua 15:18-21; Joshua 17:8), Isaac (Genesis 26:3), and Jacob (Genesis 28:13) is now fulfilled in the tribal allotments and Levitical cities just described. The word nishba (swore by oath) frames the fulfillment in terms of divine fidelity: this is not contingent provision but oath-keeping.
44: vayanuach YHWH lahem misaviv kekhol asher nishba la'avotam: ‘the LORD gave them rest on every side, just as he had sworn to their ancestors’ — the second fulfillment declaration introduces menuha (rest, the same word as Deuteronomy 12:9 and the Sabbath-rest motif that runs through the OT). The promise was not just land but rest in the land — cessation of the warfare and displacement that characterized the wilderness generation. The rest here is partial and provisional (the incompletely conquered Canaanites remain); Hebrews 4:8 argues that the incompleteness of Joshua’s rest proves a greater rest remains.
45: lo nafal davar mikol hadavar hatov asher diber YHWH el beit Yisrael hakol ba: ‘not one word of all the good things that YHWH spoke concerning the house of Israel failed; all came to pass’ — the third and most comprehensive fulfillment declaration. Lo nafal davar (not one word fell) uses the verb nafal (fall) metaphorically: words are things that stand or fall; YHWH’s words stand forever (Isaiah 40:8: “the word of our God will stand forever”). Joshua 21:45 is the OT’s most comprehensive testimony to divine promise-faithfulness, and 2 Corinthians 1:20 (“all the promises of God find their Yes in him”) is its NT equivalent and ultimate basis.
